Member-only story

Every one of these JavaScript and TypeScript mistakes looked completely correct during code review. Then production turned them into bugs, outages, and hours of debugging nobody saw coming.

5 min read
Just now

--

👉Friend link :
1. Using == Instead of ===
Every JavaScript developer knows this one.
Yet it still sneaks into production.
Consider this:
console.log(0 == false);Output:
trueNow this:
console.log("" == false);Output:
trueAnd this:
console.log(null == undefined);Output:
trueJavaScript tries to “help” by converting values before comparison.
Unfortunately, its definition of helping is questionable.
Using strict equality avoids these surprises.
console.log(0 === false);
